1
v  Given a real world recording containing a speech signal,  the
7  field of speech enhancement focuses on improving its quality
1  and intelligibility by suppressing noise and reverberation arti-
5  facts. Noise sources in form of additional interfering speech
4  signals,  such as in the so-called cocktail-party problem [1],
1
.  are especially challenging due to their non-stationary temporal-
5  spectral properties. Furthermore, such scenarios require addi-
0  tional information about the target speaker in order to resolve
5  the ambiguity regarding which speaker to enhance and which
2  speakers to suppress.  This particular problem setting in the
:
realm of speech enhancement is referred to as target speaker ex-
v
i  traction (TSE) and defined by the task of identifying, disentan-
gling and enhancing a target speech signal from a multi-speaker
X
r  recording based on a speaker-specific cue. While a variety of
a  cue modalities have been proposed for this purpose, see e.g. [2]
for an overview, spatial information becomes especially pop-
ular in the case of a stationary speaker and the availability of
recordings from a microphone array. Commonly under the as-
sumption that contributing speech sources are spatially distin-
guishable by their azimuth orientation to the array, a spatially
selective filter (SSF) can be employed and steered in the tar-
get’s direction to extract  the desired speech signal [3, 4, 5].
Recently proposed data-driven implementations have gained a
great deal of attention by demonstrating very high spatial selec-
tivity with only a limited amount of microphones [3, 4, 6, 7]. By
joint exploitation of spatial, temporal and spectral information,
such deep non-linear SSFs drastically improve the enhancement
performance over traditional linear filtering approaches as e.g.

Selective Filters for Weakly Guided
kers in Dynamic Scenarios
Timo Gerkmann
ersity of Hamburg, Germany
, timo.gerkmann@uni-hamburg.de
the minimum-variance distortionless response (MVDR) beam-
former [8]. However, due to the resulting high spatial selectiv-
ity, they also result in an increased dependency on the provision
of accurate directional information [3, 6].
A recording setup such as a seated conference meeting with
a centered microphone array [9] can legitimate the assumption
of stationary and directionally distinct speaker locations. The
training procedure can even be adapted to increase the robust-
ness of SSFs against small perturbations such as minor cue
inaccuracies or head movement of a speaker [3].  However,
in more general settings such as e.g.  the dinner party sce-
nario considered in [10], participants can move freely within the
recording environment and the prior assumptions are no longer
valid. With the majority of data-driven SSFs utilizing tempo-
ral context to extract the target’s speech signal [3, 4, 5, 6], the
change from a stationary to a dynamic acoustic scenario bears
a significant domain shift.  To counteract  this effect,  recent
works include non-stationary speaker locations during train-
ing by abruptly changing their positions over the duration of
the recording [5, 11]. Although spatialization with such piece-
wise constant trajectories is far less computationally expensive
than simulating continuous motion, their discontinuity leads to
a poor generalization in real recordings as demonstrated in [12]
for the task of sound source localization (SSL). The arising ne-
cessity of time-dependent directional cues bears an additional
challenge, since they are usually not available in practical ap-
plications. While [3, 5] have used an upstream SSL to estimate
the speaker locations for the task of speech separation, oracle
information is necessary to disentangle speaker trajectories in
case two speakers pass each other [5]. Furthermore, during such
indeterminate speaker arrangements a SSF cannot differentiate
the overlapping speech signals solely based on their relative az-
imuth orientations toward the microphone array, see the visu-
alization in [5]. Whereas enriching the cue with additional po-
sitional information [6, 7] or changing its modality to include
the target’s temporal-spectral characteristics e.g. by utilizing an
enrollment utterance [11, 13, 14] can help to resolve the ambi-
guity, it greatly limits the applicability in real-world scenarios.
Instead of relying on accurate time-dependent directional
cues, which we refer to as strong guidance, we propose a weakly
guided TSE method which solely depends on the speaker’s ini-
tial position. By creating a synthetic dataset modeling continu-
ous movement, we demonstrate the significant performance im-
provement of SSFs in dynamic scenarios and showcase their
capability to resolve spatial ambiguities by learning to differen-
tiate temporal-spectral patterns.

Finally, by introducing a joint
training strategy, we can not only report strong results com-
bined with our own deep tracking neural network (NN), but also
paired with a very simple and inaccurate tracking algorithm..

Steering Deep Non-Linear Spatially
Extraction of Moving Spea
Jakob Kienegger,
Signal Processing (SP), Univ
jakob.kienegger@uni-hamburg.de
Abstract
5  Recent speaker extraction methods using deep non-linear spa-
2  tial filtering perform exceptionally well when the target direc-
0  tion is known and stationary. However, spatially dynamic sce-
2  narios are considerably more challenging due to time-varying
 
spatial  features and arising ambiguities, e.g.  when moving
y
speakers cross. While in a static scenario it may be easy for
a
a user to point to the target’s direction, manually tracking a
M  moving speaker is impractical.  Instead of relying on accurate
 
time-dependent directional cues, which we refer to as strong
0
guidance, in this paper we propose a weakly guided extraction
2
   method solely depending on the target’s initial position to cope
 
]  with spatial dynamic scenarios. By incorporating our own deep
S  tracking algorithm and developing a joint training strategy on
a synthetic dataset, we demonstrate the proficiency of our ap-
A
.  proach in resolving spatial ambiguities and even outperform a
s  mismatched, but strongly guided extraction method.
s
Index Terms: multi-channel, moving source, spatially selective
e
e  filter, target speaker extraction, direction of arrival estimation

1.  Introduction
1
v  Given a real world recording containing a speech signal,  the
7  field of speech enhancement focuses on improving its quality
1  and intelligibility by suppressing noise and reverberation arti-
5  facts. Noise sources in form of additional interfering speech
4  signals,  such as in the so-called cocktail-party problem [1],
1
.  are especially challenging due to their non-stationary temporal-
5  spectral properties. Furthermore, such scenarios require addi-
0  tional information about the target speaker in order to resolve
5  the ambiguity regarding which speaker to enhance and which
2  speakers to suppress.  This particular problem setting in the
:
realm of speech enhancement is referred to as target speaker ex-
v
i  traction (TSE) and defined by the task of identifying, disentan-
gling and enhancing a target speech signal from a multi-speaker
X
r  recording based on a speaker-specific cue. While a variety of
a  cue modalities have been proposed for this purpose, see e.g. [2]
for an overview, spatial information becomes especially pop-
ular in the case of a stationary speaker and the availability of
recordings from a microphone array. Commonly under the as-
sumption that contributing speech sources are spatially distin-
guishable by their azimuth orientation to the array, a spatially
selective filter (SSF) can be employed and steered in the tar-
get’s direction to extract  the desired speech signal [3, 4, 5].
Recently proposed data-driven implementations have gained a
great deal of attention by demonstrating very high spatial selec-
tivity with only a limited amount of microphones [3, 4, 6, 7]. By
joint exploitation of spatial, temporal and spectral information,
such deep non-linear SSFs drastically improve the enhancement
performance over traditional linear filtering approaches as e.g.

Selective Filters for Weakly Guided
kers in Dynamic Scenarios
Timo Gerkmann
ersity of Hamburg, Germany
, timo.gerkmann@uni-hamburg.de
the minimum-variance distortionless response (MVDR) beam-
former [8]. However, due to the resulting high spatial selectiv-
ity, they also result in an increased dependency on the provision
of accurate directional information [3, 6].
A recording setup such as a seated conference meeting with
a centered microphone array [9] can legitimate the assumption
of stationary and directionally distinct speaker locations. The
training procedure can even be adapted to increase the robust-
ness of SSFs against small perturbations such as minor cue
inaccuracies or head movement of a speaker [3].  However,
in more general settings such as e.g.  the dinner party sce-
nario considered in [10], participants can move freely within the
recording environment and the prior assumptions are no longer
valid. With the majority of data-driven SSFs utilizing tempo-
ral context to extract the target’s speech signal [3, 4, 5, 6], the
change from a stationary to a dynamic acoustic scenario bears
a significant domain shift.  To counteract  this effect,  recent
works include non-stationary speaker locations during train-
ing by abruptly changing their positions over the duration of
the recording [5, 11]. Although spatialization with such piece-
wise constant trajectories is far less computationally expensive
than simulating continuous motion, their discontinuity leads to
a poor generalization in real recordings as demonstrated in [12]
for the task of sound source localization (SSL). The arising ne-
cessity of time-dependent directional cues bears an additional
challenge, since they are usually not available in practical ap-
plications.

While [3, 5] have used an upstream SSL to estimate
the speaker locations for the task of speech separation, oracle
information is necessary to disentangle speaker trajectories in
case two speakers pass each other [5]. Furthermore, during such
indeterminate speaker arrangements a SSF cannot differentiate
the overlapping speech signals solely based on their relative az-
imuth orientations toward the microphone array, see the visu-
alization in [5]. Whereas enriching the cue with additional po-
sitional information [6, 7] or changing its modality to include
the target’s temporal-spectral characteristics e.g. by utilizing an
enrollment utterance [11, 13, 14] can help to resolve the ambi-
guity, it greatly limits the applicability in real-world scenarios.
Instead of relying on accurate time-dependent directional
cues, which we refer to as strong guidance, we propose a weakly
guided TSE method which solely depends on the speaker’s ini-
tial position. By creating a synthetic dataset modeling continu-
ous movement, we demonstrate the significant performance im-
provement of SSFs in dynamic scenarios and showcase their
capability to resolve spatial ambiguities by learning to differen-
tiate temporal-spectral patterns. Finally, by introducing a joint
training strategy, we can not only report strong results com-
bined with our own deep tracking neural network (NN), but also
paired with a very simple and inaccurate tracking algorithm..

guishable by their azimuth orientation to the array, a spatially

great deal of attention by demonstrating very high spatial selec-
tivity with only a limited amount of microphones [3, 4, 6, 7]. By

kers in Dynamic Scenarios
Timo Gerkmann
ersity of Hamburg, Germany
, timo.gerkmann@uni-hamburg.de
the minimum-variance distortionless response (MVDR) beam-
former [8]. However, due to the resulting high spatial selectiv-
ity, they also result in an increased dependency on the provision
of accurate directional information [3, 6].
A recording setup such as a seated conference meeting with
a centered microphone array [9] can legitimate the assumption
of stationary and directionally distinct speaker locations. The
training procedure can even be adapted to increase the robust-
ness of SSFs against small perturbations such as minor cue
inaccuracies or head movement of a speaker [3].  However,
in more general settings such as e.g.  the dinner party sce-
nario considered in [10], participants can move freely within the
recording environment and the prior assumptions are no longer
valid. With the majority of data-driven SSFs utilizing tempo-
ral context to extract the target’s speech signal [3, 4, 5, 6], the
change from a stationary to a dynamic acoustic scenario bears
a significant domain shift.  To counteract  this effect,  recent
works include non-stationary speaker locations during train-
ing by abruptly changing their positions over the duration of
the recording [5, 11]. Although spatialization with such piece-
wise constant trajectories is far less computationally expensive
than simulating continuous motion, their discontinuity leads to
a poor generalization in real recordings as demonstrated in [12]
for the task of sound source localization (SSL). The arising ne-
cessity of time-dependent directional cues bears an additional
challenge, since they are usually not available in practical ap-
plications.

While [3, 5] have used an upstream SSL to estimate
the speaker locations for the task of speech separation, oracle
information is necessary to disentangle speaker trajectories in
case two speakers pass each other [5]. Furthermore, during such
indeterminate speaker arrangements a SSF cannot differentiate
the overlapping speech signals solely based on their relative az-
imuth orientations toward the microphone array, see the visu-
alization in [5]. Whereas enriching the cue with additional po-
sitional information [6, 7] or changing its modality to include
the target’s temporal-spectral characteristics e.g. by utilizing an
enrollment utterance [11, 13, 14] can help to resolve the ambi-
guity, it greatly limits the applicability in real-world scenarios.

cues, which we refer to as strong guidance, we propose a weakly
guided TSE method which solely depends on the speaker’s ini-
tial position. By creating a synthetic dataset modeling continu-
ous movement, we demonstrate the significant performance im-
provement of SSFs in dynamic scenarios and showcase their
capability to resolve spatial ambiguities by learning to differen-
tiate temporal-spectral patterns. Finally, by introducing a joint
training strategy, we can not only report strong results com-
bined with our own deep tracking neural network (NN), but also
paired with a very simple and inaccurate tracking algorithm..

r  recording based on a speaker-specific cue. While a variety of
a  cue modalities have been proposed for this purpose, see e.g. [2]
for an overview, spatial information becomes especially pop-
ular in the case of a stationary speaker and the availability of
recordings from a microphone array. Commonly under the as-
sumption that contributing speech sources are spatially distin-
guishable by their azimuth orientation to the array, a spatially
selective filter (SSF) can be employed and steered in the tar-
get’s direction to extract  the desired speech signal [3, 4, 5].
Recently proposed data-driven implementations have gained a
great deal of attention by demonstrating very high spatial selec-
tivity with only a limited amount of microphones [3, 4, 6, 7]. By
joint exploitation of spatial, temporal and spectral information,
such deep non-linear SSFs drastically improve the enhancement
performance over traditional linear filtering approaches as e.g.

Selective Filters for Weakly Guided
kers in Dynamic Scenarios
Timo Gerkmann
ersity of Hamburg, Germany
, timo.gerkmann@uni-hamburg.de
the minimum-variance distortionless response (MVDR) beam-
former [8]. However, due to the resulting high spatial selectiv-
ity, they also result in an increased dependency on the provision
of accurate directional information [3, 6].
A recording setup such as a seated conference meeting with
a centered microphone array [9] can legitimate the assumption
of stationary and directionally distinct speaker locations. The
training procedure can even be adapted to increase the robust-
ness of SSFs against small perturbations such as minor cue
inaccuracies or head movement of a speaker [3].  However,
in more general settings such as e.g.  the dinner party sce-
nario considered in [10], participants can move freely within the
recording environment and the prior assumptions are no longer
valid. With the majority of data-driven SSFs utilizing tempo-
ral context to extract the target’s speech signal [3, 4, 5, 6], the
change from a stationary to a dynamic acoustic scenario bears
a significant domain shift.  To counteract  this effect,  recent
works include non-stationary speaker locations during train-
ing by abruptly changing their positions over the duration of
the recording [5, 11]. Although spatialization with such piece-
wise constant trajectories is far less computationally expensive
than simulating continuous motion, their discontinuity leads to
a poor generalization in real recordings as demonstrated in [12]
for the task of sound source localization (SSL). The arising ne-
cessity of time-dependent directional cues bears an additional
challenge, since they are usually not available in practical ap-
plications.

While [3, 5] have used an upstream SSL to estimate
the speaker locations for the task of speech separation, oracle
information is necessary to disentangle speaker trajectories in
case two speakers pass each other [5]. Furthermore, during such
indeterminate speaker arrangements a SSF cannot differentiate
the overlapping speech signals solely based on their relative az-
imuth orientations toward the microphone array, see the visu-
alization in [5]. Whereas enriching the cue with additional po-
sitional information [6, 7] or changing its modality to include
the target’s temporal-spectral characteristics e.g. by utilizing an
enrollment utterance [11, 13, 14] can help to resolve the ambi-
guity, it greatly limits the applicability in real-world scenarios.
Instead of relying on accurate time-dependent directional
cues, which we refer to as strong guidance, we propose a weakly
guided TSE method which solely depends on the speaker’s ini-
tial position. By creating a synthetic dataset modeling continu-
ous movement, we demonstrate the significant performance im-
provement of SSFs in dynamic scenarios and showcase their
capability to resolve spatial ambiguities by learning to differen-
tiate temporal-spectral patterns. Finally, by introducing a joint
training strategy, we can not only report strong results com-
bined with our own deep tracking neural network (NN), but also
paired with a very simple and inaccurate tracking algorithm.

2. Problem definition
2.1. Target speaker extraction
Let the multi-channel observation Y be modeled as additive
mixture of reverberant target speech X with noise V. The lat-
ter contains interfering speech sources as well as additive en-
vironmental and measurement noise.  In the short-time Fourier
transform (STFT) domain, this acoustic setup is resembled by
Ytk = Xtk +Vtk ,  (1)
with t and k denoting frame and frequency bins respectively
and vectorization conducted over the channel dimension.  In
this work, we define the task of TSE as the recovery of the di-
rect path propagated speech signal Stk, also referred to as dry
speech signal [3], at a predefined reference microphone. While
a vast amount of methods have been proposed for this task, we
will focus on deep NN algorithms computing a complex-valued
mask Mtk to extract the target speech signal S(cid:98)tk by multipli-
0
cation with a reference channel of the input Ytk
0
S(cid:98)tk =MtkYtk .  (2)
The data-driven estimation of time-frequency mask Mtk con-
ditioned on a target specific cue is the center of this work.
2.2. Spatially selective filter
Spatially selective filters (SSFs) exploit positional cues to dis-
tinguish the target’s speech signal  from interfering sources.
Since accurate positional  information  in  three-dimensional
space is usually not available, only the relative azimuth orienta-
tion of the target speaker towards the microphone array is com-
monly utilized for this purpose [3, 4, 5]. To some degree this can
be legitimized in case the inter-microphone distance is negligi-
ble towards the distance between target speaker and recording
array.  On the one hand the validity of the far-field approxi-
mation [8] increases, reducing the influence of distance on the
signal characteristics. Furthermore, a change in speaker height
only results in a slight change in elevation angle, which leaves
the azimuth direction as the most distinct spatial feature. How-
ever, there have been extensions proposed to incorporate infor-
mation about elevation as well as distance into the extraction
process [6, 7].  In this work we abide by common convention
and constrain the cue on the azimuth angle, which we will refer
to as direction of arrival (DoA) in the following. Thus, the task
of the SSF lies in computing the time-frequency mask Mtk in
(2) from the multi-channel input Ytk conditioned on the DoA θ.
In most realizations, the latter is provided as a one-hot encoded
vector z corresponding to a specific spatial discretization in Θ
equidistant angular regions [3, 5, 6]. While in the majority of
prior work this cue is time-independent and a stationary setting
is assumed [3, 4, 5, 6], we explicitly include motion to target
and interfering speakers, thus, necessitating time-dependent di-
rectional information θt, as indicated by the index t.
3. Proposed method
3.1. Weakly guided target speaker extraction
Instead of relying on accurate continuous prior directional in-
formation, we relax this constraint and only assume knowl-
edge about the initial starting direction θ0 of the target speaker.
In order to retain compatibility with conventional SSFs like
[3, 4, 5, 6], we propose to incorporate an upstream tracking al-

In contrast to strongly guided TSE, our method

gorithm to estimate the evolution of the target’s DoA θt based
on θ0.  In analogy to TSE, we will refer to this task as target
speaker tracking (TST) and avoid the term target speaker local-
ization, as it is commonly paired with audio cues [13, 15, 16].
Figure 1 displays the resulting TSE pipeline in form of a con-
catenation of an upstream TST and downstream SSF algorithm.
3.2. Target speaker tracking
The general problem of estimating and tracking the direction of
a moving source is a well-established field of research. Classi-
cal model-based approaches such as Kalman filter (KF) [18, 19]
or particle filter (PF) [20, 21] build on an underlying statistical
framework and provide robust and computationally efficient al-
gorithms. However, the performance of such algorithms sub-
stantially decreases in challenging noisy and reverberant sce-
narios [22]. While improvements are possible by incorpora-
tion of data-driven methods [23, 24], in case enough training
data is available, recent trends are moving toward fully neu-
ral approaches.  Especially deep recurrent architectures show
great promise in the related field of SSL by learning intricate
dependencies between observation and the temporal evolution
of the DoA [17, 25, 26].  In this work we use the established
causal SSL model proposed by Bohlender et al.  [17] as start-
ing point and modify it for the purpose of TST. Due to a con-
volutional spatial preprocessing followed by a unidirectional
LSTM layer, it is commonly referred to as CNN/LSTM [5, 17].
In its original implementation the DoA estimation is encoded
in a classification problem in fixed angular steps, which is a
common approach for data-driven SSL algorithms [3, 27, 28].
While the resulting spatial quantization can lead to estimation
inaccuracies, in our specific use-case the output modality per-
fectly matches the one-hot encoding zt of the downstream SSF.

To modify the architecture towards tracking a specific target
speaker, we change the original output activation from Sigmoid
to Softmax [27], making it interpretable as posterior probability
p(θt|Y1:t, θ0). We then estimate the target’s DoA θ(cid:98)t according
to the maximum a posteriori (MAP) decision rule
θ = argmax p(θ |Y  , θ ) .

In order to inform the NN about the initial DoA θ0, we choose to
adopt the initialization technique employed in the FT-JNF archi-
tecture [3]. Specifically, we use the one-hot encoded DoA rep-
resentation zt transformed by a linear layer directly as initial-
ization of the LSTM in the TST network. Finally, we replace the
original input features in [17] with the complex multi-channel
spectrogram, matching both FT-JNF and SSL proposed in [3].
The resulting deep TST architecture is illustrated in fig. 2.
4. Experiments
4.1. Dataset
For training and evaluation we create a synthetic dataset with
reverberant two-speaker mixtures.  Specifically, we use utter-
ances from the LibriSpeech [29] corpus, convolve them with
simulated room impulse responses (RIRs) based on the image
method [30] and then combine them according to the recipe in
Libri2Mix [31]. Since no additional noise is present in the mix-
ture, the interfering signal Vtk in (1) consists of the early and
late reflections of the target speech signal and the reverberant
interfering speech. The acoustic room setup closely follows the
work from Tesch et al.  [3]. Hence, we choose a circular array
of 10 cm diameter as recording device, which consists of three
uniformly spaced omnidirectional microphones. We adhere to
the reverberation times between 0.2 s and 0.5 s as well as the
same simulation setup for the shoe-box rooms. At the start of
each mixture, target and interfering speaker are separated by at
◦
least 10  and at a distance between 0.8 to 1.2m from the ar-
ray. The distance from the array as well as the height of the
speaker is kept constant over the duration of each mixture while
the azimuth direction may vary.

4.2. Network architectures and training details
Spatially selective filter To test our proposed methods with
a strong SSF baseline we choose FT-JNF proposed by Tesch et
al.  in [3]. Due to its exceptional spatial filtering capabilities
paired with a conceptually simple and lightweight architecture,
it has seen great popularity in a variety of applications [34, 35,
36].  In order to retain causality, we slightly deviate from [3]
and replace the last B-LSTM layer by an unidirectional LSTM
of twice its initial parameters to roughly preserve the intended
model size. We train FT-JNF with an initial learning rate of
−3  −2
10  and exponential decay of 10  according to the joint time
and frequency domain loss function in [3].
Target speaker tracking To cope with the challenging mo-
tion dynamics  in our dataset, we utilize our proposed ar-
chitecture in section 3.2 as upstream TST method with the
parametrization shown in fig. 2. We train the NN to minimize
the cross-entropy towards the one-hot encoded ground truth
−4
DoA zt with an initial learning rate of 10  and exponential
−2
decay of 10  .  To give a comparison with a traditional ap-
proach, we choose a PF due to its popularity and algorithmic
simplicity. Specifically, we employ the algorithm in [21] using
the output power of a delay-and-sum (DaS) beamformer as like-
lihood, which we will refer to as DaS-PF in the following. To
strengthen this baseline, we employ the motion model in (4) and
match the perturbation’s standard deviation σ to the dataset.
Training All NNs are trained on mixtures of 5 s length
while resampling the randomized acoustic setup from sec-
tion 4.1 including the speaker trajectories in each step.  The
latter becomes feasible due to the GPU accelerated implemen-
tation of the image method in [37]. We use spatial discretization
◦
of 2 which results in a total number of 180 spatial regions Θ.
We employ two different training strategies for our proposed
TSE pipeline in fig. 1.  In the first we train the NNs indepen-
dently for a total of 100 and 50 epochs regarding the SSF and
TST architectures respectively. The second strategy is to train
the pipeline in an end-to-end fashion, consequently, we drop the
argmax operation in (3) to retain differentiability.

For compara-
bility, we pre-train the SSF and TST NNs independently for 75
and 25 epochs and then continue for the remaining 25 epochs
on their concatenation regarding the loss of the FT-JNF.

5. Results
We evaluate the TSE performance w.r.t. distortions, perceptual
quality and intelligibility via the metrics SI-SDR [38], PESQ
[39] and ESTOI [40] respectively. Table 1 displays the results
using FT-JNF with different tracking and training strategies for
three levels of speaker dynamics, complemented by fig. 4 re-
garding SI-SDR improvement with a refined resolution.
Strongly guided speaker extraction To investigate the im-
pact of moving speakers on the extraction capabilities of a SSF,
we supply FT-JNF with continuous ground truth DoA informa-
tion. When training in a stationary setting, FT-JNF displays
a sharp performance decline across all metrics with increasing
speaker motion (1). The same network but trained on a dynamic
setup, in which we uniformly draw the expected azimuth shift
E{|∆θt|} from the evaluation interval, is only slightly affected
by the speaker’s motion and results in improved metrics for all
non-stationary cases (2). Since FT-JNF optimized in a motion-
less environment (1) can fully rely on distinct and stationary
spatial features, the network is bound to overfit on this specific
setup achieving a very high spatial selectivity [3]. However,
when dropping this constraint, the SSF is not able to cope with
the increasingly challenging acoustic characteristics as well as
incapable of handling overlapping speakers due to solely rely-
ing on the provided spatial cue. On the other hand, as shown
in fig. 6,  training the identical network in a dynamic setting,
FT-JNF is able to resolve the ambiguity and attenuate the in-
terfering speaker sufficiently. This demonstrates that although
being conditioned on a spatial cue, SSFs are also capable of
distinguishing and leveraging temporal-spectral characteristics.
Weakly guided speaker extraction Instead of continuous
oracle DoA information,  our proposed weakly guided TSE
pipeline only relies on the target’s initial DoA by utilizing
an upstream TST system.  To compare both tracking algo-
rithms discussed in this work, we visualize sample trajecto-

ries in fig. 3 and report frame-wise accuracy (ACC) with a 5°
margin [15, 16] and angular error (AE) in fig. 5. Although the
DaS-PF utilizes the motion model with oracle parametrization,
which explains the perfect scores in the stationary case, the per-
formance decreases rapidly with increasing speaker movement.
While our proposed network shows a much stronger and consis-
tent overall performance, it also follows the same trend. For this
reason the simple concatenation of SSF and TST methods leads
to a significant performance decrease in all cases (3, 4, 6, 7).
However, with our proposed joint training strategy, we are able
to counteract this effect and even surpass the strongly guided
TSE configuration in (1) regarding distortion and speech qual-
ity metrics for the non-stationary setups, underlining the im-
portance of a dynamic dataset during training.

estimates, the FT-JNF is able to adapt with our joint training
strategy and greatly improve across all metrics, see also fig. 4.

6. Conclusion
In this work we proposed a weakly guided TSE pipeline which
solely requires information about the target’s initial direction.
By developing a synthetic dataset with continuous speaker
movement, we could not only demonstrate the significant per-
formance improvement of a commonly used SSF in dynamic
environments, but also its capability to resolve spatial ambigu-
ities by learning to differentiate temporal-spectral patterns. Fi-
nally, by introducing a joint training strategy, we could surpass
the performance of a mismatched, but strongly guided system
with our own deep tracking NN and also illustrate how SSFs can
leverage highly inaccurate directional cues and achieve compet-
itive performance even with a very simple tracking algorithm.
